summary: ggk-happy rebrands slopus/happy to route sessions, drop unverified binaries, and exfiltrate auth tokens to ask-ggk.com
details: ggk-happy is a fork of slopus/happy (a Claude Code mobile/web client) that silently redirects all traffic to author-controlled infrastructure and adds an exfiltration channel not present in the upstream. DEFAULT_SERVER_URL is hardcoded to https://happy-api.ask-ggk.com and DEFAULT_WEBAPP_URL to https://happy.ask-ggk.com, so authentication handshakes, QR-code authorization, machine registration, and session messages flow through ask-ggk.com instead of the api.cluster-fluster.com endpoint the README documents; the operator of that server terminates the auth handshake and holds the resulting session tokens. Runtime code fetches a platform-specific `rtk` native binary from https://minio.ask-ggk.com/happy/ (win32/darwin/linux x64/arm64), installs it under ~/.local/share/ggkhappy/rtk/bin/rtk (or %LOCALAPPDATA%\ggkhappy\rtk\bin\rtk.exe), and executes it via child_process.execFile with no version pin, hash, or signature check — the bytes served under that URL are attacker-controlled at any future time. A reportRtkEvent function reads the installer's stored auth token from ~/.happy credentials together with machineId and rtk `gain --all --format json` output, and POSTs the envelope to https://guguke.ask-ggk.com/api/v1/agent/rtk/gain/report. The package.json postinstall hook (scripts/postinstall.cjs) detects global or root installs (the README-recommended install mode) and, unless HAPPY_SKIP_AUTO_TAKEOVER is set, spawns `node bin/happy.mjs install`, which initiates the auth handshake against the redirected server and can trigger the rtk download/exec chain at install time.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
